content.text: Radar Governance: Making the Tech Radar a Living Artefact

A technology radar that is published once and never revisited is worse than no radar at all — it gives engineers false confidence that stale recommendations are current. Governance is the discipline that keeps the radar honest: a defined cycle for proposing, evaluating, voting on, and publishing ring changes, plus a clear escalation path for urgent out-of-band events such as a critical CVE or an unexpected vendor end-of-life announcement.

This document defines a production-ready governance model sized for a mid-to-large enterprise GenAI platform team. It covers the quarterly cadence, the roles that participate, the artefacts produced at each stage, and the automation hooks that reduce manual overhead.

attrs.alt: Tech Radar quarterly governance cycle — nomination, spike, vote, draft, publish phases

content.text: Four radar publications per year matches the enterprise project planning rhythm — teams planning a new quarter can consult the freshly published radar when making technology choices. Each publication follows a 10-week internal cycle:
YAML blip nominations in PR to tech-radar repo
Six-dimension score computed for each blip
Draft radar HTML/YAML published to internal preview URL
Radar merged to main; announcement sent

The 10-week cycle is the minimum viable cadence. Shorter cycles (e.g. 6 weeks) reduce the time available for Assess spikes, which typically take 2 weeks to run rigorously. Longer cycles (e.g. 16 weeks) cause the radar to lag the ecosystem — a technology can go from preview to production-ready between publications.
Submit blip nominations with supporting evidence and a draft ring suggestion
Run Assess spikes; maintain domain knowledge for one quadrant (e.g. vector stores)
Review scores, hear dissents, confirm ring assignments via majority vote
Merge nominations, produce draft, manage publication pipeline, send announcement
Approve or veto any Adopt-ring entry that touches data handling or external API calls

Nominations arrive as pull requests to a tech-radar Git repository. The PR adds or modifies a single entry in the YAML blip registry. Requiring a PR enforces a written record of who nominated what and why, and gives the wider engineering community visibility before the ARB vote.

# tech-radar/nominations/2025-Q3/guardrails-ai.yaml
id: guardrails-ai
quadrant: frameworks-and-libraries
proposed_ring: Trial           # nominator's suggestion
current_ring: Assess           # or "new" if not yet on radar

title: "Guardrails AI — output validation framework"
description: >
  Guardrails AI provides RAIL-spec-driven validators for LLM output fields
  (type coercion, regex match, PII detection, custom validators). Evaluated
  in Q2 on a document-extraction pipeline; p99 overhead was 40ms for a
  10-field validator.

evidence:
  - type: internal_pilot
    link: "https://confluence.internal/display/AI/guardrails-pilot-Q2-2025"
    summary: "Met quality gate; latency overhead acceptable"
  - type: external_case_study
    link: "https://guardrailsai.com/docs/use-cases"

nominator: alice@example.com
date: 2025-07-01

The PR template enforces the required fields and runs a YAML lint check in CI. Nominations without evidence links are automatically labelled 'needs-evidence' and excluded from the cycle's vote until evidence is added.

Nominations proposing a move from Assess to Trial require an executed spike. A spike is a time-boxed (two-week) technical evaluation run by the technology owner in an isolated environment using a representative workload from production logs.

# Spike Report: {{ technology }} — {{ date }}

## Summary (≤ 5 lines)
<!-- What was evaluated, what was the primary finding, what is the recommendation -->

## Setup
- Version evaluated: x.y.z
- Environment: isolated dev cluster (no prod credentials)
- Workload: 500 requests sampled from prod logs (anonymised)

## Functional findings
| Test case | Result | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Core use case | PASS/FAIL | |
| Edge case: empty input | PASS/FAIL | |
| Error handling | PASS/FAIL | |

## Non-functional measurements
| Metric | Measured | Target | Status |
|--------|----------|--------|--------|
| p99 latency (ms) | 45 | <100 | PASS |
| Memory overhead (MB) | 28 | <100 | PASS |
| CPU overhead (%) | 3 | <10 | PASS |

## Security findings
- CVE scan: clean (pip-audit output attached)
- License: Apache 2.0 (compatible with enterprise policy)
- Data handling: no credentials in logs confirmed

## Ring recommendation
Trial — meets functional and NFR gates; recommend bounded pilot on team X

## Open questions
- Does the validator compose cleanly with LangChain output parsers?

The technology owner submits a completed scoring sheet alongside the spike report. The ARB reviews scores in a 90-minute meeting, hears brief presentations on controversial entries, and votes by majority. The chair (radar editor) has a casting vote in the event of a tie.

All score sheets and override reasons are committed to the tech-radar repository. This means every ring assignment is traceable to a specific evaluator and a documented justification — important for audit trails and for onboarding new ARB members.

After the ARB vote, the radar editor generates the draft radar from the YAML registry and publishes it to an internal preview URL for a one-week comment window. Comments arrive as GitHub PR review comments on the radar YAML files — not via Slack or email, to keep the discussion record alongside the artefact.

# .github/workflows/publish-radar.yml
name: Publish Tech Radar

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "tech-radar/blips/**"
      - "tech-radar/radar.yaml"

jobs:
  build-and-publish:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Validate YAML schema
        run: python scripts/validate_radar_schema.py tech-radar/

      - name: Compute rings from scores
        run: python scripts/compute_rings.py tech-radar/ > tech-radar/computed-rings.json

      - name: Generate radar HTML
        run: node scripts/build_radar_html.mjs

      - name: Generate diff from previous radar
        run: python scripts/radar_diff.py --prev releases/last.json --next tech-radar/computed-rings.json

      - name: Publish to internal docs site
        run: |
          aws s3 sync dist/radar/ s3://internal-docs/tech-radar/ --delete
          aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id $CF_DIST_ID --paths "/tech-radar/*"
        env:
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.RADAR_PUBLISH_KEY }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.RADAR_PUBLISH_SECRET }}
          CF_DIST_ID: ${{ secrets.RADAR_CF_DIST }}

      - name: Send announcement email
        run: python scripts/send_radar_announcement.py --diff tech-radar/diff.json
        env:
          SENDGRID_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SENDGRID_KEY }}

Subject: Tech Radar Q3 2025 Published — 4 ring changes

## What changed this quarter

### Moved to Adopt
- **LangGraph** (Frameworks) — graduated from Trial after successful pilot on
  the claims-processing agent. Production-grade as of v0.2.

### Moved to Trial
- **Guardrails AI** (Frameworks) — Assess spike met all NFR gates. Run a
  bounded pilot before adopting in new projects.

### Moved to Hold
- **GPT-4-turbo-preview** (Platforms) — superseded by GPT-4o; EOL announced
  for 2025-12-01. Migrate open projects by 2025-11-01.

### New on radar (Assess)
- **Gemini 2.0 Flash** (Platforms) — promising latency profile; no SLA yet.
  Spike planned for Q4.

## Links
- Full radar: https://internal-docs.example.com/tech-radar/
- Change log: https://github.com/example/tech-radar/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md
- Questions: #platform-architecture Slack channel
Not all ring changes can wait for the quarterly cycle. Three categories of events trigger an immediate out-of-band update:
Critical CVE (CVSS ≥ 9.0) in an Adopt/Trial entry
Move to Hold; open migration backlog; notify security team
Vendor announces unexpected EoL < 90 days out
Move to Hold; identify migration target; notify affected teams
A Hold entry's successor reaches Adopt readiness mid-quarter
Promote successor to Adopt; no ARB vote needed if spike is already complete

Out-of-band updates bypass the nomination and spike phases but still require a PR to the tech-radar repository with a documented reason. The CISO delegate must approve any security-triggered Hold within 48 hours.

from datetime import date, timedelta
import httpx

RADAR_REPO_API = "https://api.github.com/repos/example/tech-radar"

def raise_out_of_band_pr(
    blip_id: str,
    new_ring: str,
    reason: str,
    token: str,
) -> str:
    branch = f"oob/{blip_id}-hold-{date.today().isoformat()}"
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json"}

    # Get current SHA of main
    r = httpx.get(f"{RADAR_REPO_API}/git/ref/heads/main", headers=headers)
    sha = r.json()["object"]["sha"]

    # Create branch
    httpx.post(f"{RADAR_REPO_API}/git/refs", headers=headers, json={
        "ref": f"refs/heads/{branch}", "sha": sha
    })

    # Update blip YAML (simplified — real implementation reads, patches, re-writes)
    patch_content = f"ring: {new_ring}\nout_of_band_reason: >\n  {reason}\nout_of_band_date: {date.today().isoformat()}\n"

    # Open PR
    pr = httpx.post(f"{RADAR_REPO_API}/pulls", headers=headers, json={
        "title": f"[OOB] Move {blip_id} to {new_ring}",
        "head": branch,
        "base": "main",
        "body": f"## Out-of-band update\n\n**Reason:** {reason}\n\nRequires CISO approval within 48h.",
    })
    return pr.json()["html_url"]

Q4 adds an annual full re-score to the standard 10-week cycle. Every Adopt and Trial entry is re-scored from scratch using the current six-dimension rubric — production evidence from two years ago may not reflect current alternatives, and vendor risk profiles change as companies are acquired or enter decline.
Technology owners re-score all entries in their quadrant; the previous score is visible but not pre-filled to avoid anchoring bias.
Entries that have not been modified (code or documentation) in the past 12 months are automatically flagged for mandatory re-evaluation.
The CISO delegate reviews all Adopt-ring data-handling entries annually regardless of score change.
The output of the Q4 cycle is a 'radar year-in-review' summary published alongside the normal radar — useful for leadership reporting.
Track these metrics quarterly to detect process degradation before it affects quality:
Actively solicit; radar may be perceived as closed
Do not vote on entries without a spike
Time from nomination to ARB vote
> 3 suggests the quarterly cycle is too slow
Declining views = radar not being used as a decision tool
The 10-week quarterly cycle — nomination → spike → ARB vote → draft → publish — balances thoroughness with cadence.
Nominations arrive as Git PRs with structured YAML and evidence links; nomination without evidence is automatically blocked from the vote.
Assess spikes are time-boxed two-week evaluations that produce a structured report covering functional, non-functional, and security findings.
The ARB scores each blip on a six-dimension rubric; overrides require a documented reason committed to the repository.
Critical CVEs and unexpected vendor EOL announcements trigger out-of-band Hold moves within 24–48h, bypassing the quarterly cycle.
Q4 adds an annual full re-score of all Adopt and Trial entries to catch stale placements.
Governance health is measured by nomination rate, spike completion, cycle time, out-of-band frequency, and project citation rate.
